blocks.content: The hotel was twelve minutes from the house. They'd laughed at that — the most romantic night of their lives and they were staying at the only hotel in Millhaven.
None of that mattered when the door closed behind them.
They stood in the quiet of the room and it went still.
She still had her hair up, a few pieces come loose. He crossed the room.
He reached up and pulled the last pin free. Her hair fell.
He tilted his head and pressed his lips to the side of her neck — slow, deliberate.
She exhaled. Her hands found the buttons at her back.
Morning came through the gap in the curtains in a long stripe of gold. The city — such as Millhaven was a city — was already awake, already moving.
He lay on his side and looked at her. Her hair across the pillow, her hand open beside her face.
He thought: this is what I was waiting for.
She turned toward him. He didn't look away.
